title: How to Draw Glasses: Step-by-Step Guide for Every Frame Style
description: Learn how to draw glasses step by step — from simple sketches to detailed frame styles like aviators, round, and cat-eye. Tips for beginners and artists alike.
tldr: Drawing glasses starts with understanding their basic structure: two lens shapes connected by a bridge, with temples extending from the outer edges. Master the basic front view first, then try different frame styles like aviator, round, and cat-eye. Proportions relative to the face are key.

tldrStats.label: Basic parts to draw: lenses, bridge, temples
Frame styles covered in this guide

definition.text: Drawing glasses involves sketching the basic structural elements of eyeglasses — the lens shapes, nose bridge, temples (arms), and hinges — in correct proportion to the human face. Understanding real eyewear anatomy helps artists create realistic and stylistically accurate glasses in illustrations, character design, portraits, and fashion sketches.

sections.content:
Before putting pencil to paper, it helps to understand what glasses are actually made of. Every pair has the same basic parts:

  • Lenses: The two clear (or tinted) pieces that cover the eyes. Their shape defines the overall style.
  • Rims: The frame edges that hold the lenses. Can be full rim, half rim (semi-rimless), or no rim at all (rimless).
  • Bridge: The piece connecting the two lenses, sitting on the nose. Bridges can be keyhole-shaped, saddle-shaped, or double-bar.
  • Temples: The arms that extend from the lens edges back to the ears. They usually curve at the end (temple tips).
  • Hinges: Where the temples connect to the front of the frame, allowing them to fold.
  • Nose pads: Small pads on metal frames that rest on the sides of the nose (not present on most plastic frames).

Knowing these parts helps you draw glasses that look structurally believable, even in a stylized illustration.

sections.h2: Step 1: Draw Basic Rectangular Glasses (Front View)
sections.content:
Start with the simplest shape — rectangular frames viewed from the front.

  1. Draw two rectangles side by side, with a small gap between them. Make them roughly 1.5 times wider than they are tall. Round the corners slightly — real glasses rarely have sharp 90-degree corners.
  2. Connect them with a bridge. Draw a small curved line or double line between the inner edges of the two rectangles. The bridge should sit slightly above the midpoint of the lens height.
  3. Add the temples. From each outer edge, draw a short line extending outward and slightly upward. In a front view, you only see the very beginning of the temples — they disappear behind the head.
  4. Refine the details. Add thickness to the frame by drawing a second line inside each rectangle, creating a border. Add small hinge details where the temples meet the frame front.

That's your basic pair. From here, every other style is just a variation on the lens shape.

Aviator glasses: Replace the rectangles with teardrop shapes — wider at the top, tapering to a rounded point at the bottom. The bridge is usually a double bar (two thin parallel lines). Temples are thinner than on acetate frames. Think pilot sunglasses.

Round glasses: Draw two circles instead of rectangles. Keep them perfectly round or slightly oval. A thin bridge connects them. Round frames look best with thin lines suggesting metal construction. Think John Lennon or Harry Potter.

Cat-eye glasses: Start with modified ovals that sweep upward at the outer top corners. The upward sweep is the defining feature — exaggerate it for dramatic cat-eye, keep it subtle for modern cat-eye. Thicker frames look more retro.

Browline/Clubmaster: Draw the upper half of the lens shape with a thick, bold line (acetate brow bar). The lower half uses a much thinner line (wire rim). This creates the distinctive look where the top of the frame "mimics" the eyebrow line.

Rimless glasses: Draw just the lens shapes (very lightly — these are nearly invisible). Add small mounting points where the bridge and temples attach directly to the lens edges. Rimless glasses are the hardest to draw convincingly because the lenses need to look transparent.

sections.h2: Step 3: Draw Glasses on a Face
sections.content:
Glasses on a face follow specific proportional rules:

  • Width: The frames should be roughly as wide as the face at the temple level. Not wider, not narrower.
  • Vertical position: The bridge sits on the nose bridge (between the eyes), and the lenses cover the area from just above the eyebrows to the top of the cheekbones.
  • Eye placement: Draw the eyes centered within the lens shapes. The pupils should sit roughly in the upper-middle area of each lens.
  • Bridge perspective: From a front view, the bridge is a short horizontal curve. From a three-quarter view, one lens appears slightly narrower due to perspective, and the bridge shows more dimensionality.

Common beginner mistakes:

  • Drawing glasses too small — they should be a prominent facial feature
  • Placing the bridge too high or too low on the nose
  • Forgetting that glasses have thickness — they sit in front of the face, not flat against it
  • Making both lenses identical in a three-quarter view (perspective should make one appear slightly narrower)

Once you have the basic shape right, details bring your drawing to life:

Reflections: Add a small highlight (a white curved line or shape) on each lens to suggest glass. This single detail instantly makes drawn glasses look more realistic.

Shadows: Glasses cast a subtle shadow on the face — a thin line under the lower rim on the cheeks, and possibly a shadow from the bridge on the nose.

Thickness: Show the depth of the frame, especially on three-quarter and side views. Acetate frames are thicker than metal frames.

Temple curves: When visible (side or three-quarter view), temples should curve gently over the ear. The temple tip bends downward.

Lens tint: For sunglasses, shade the lenses evenly but leave a highlight unshaded. Use cross-hatching or solid shading depending on your medium.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

sections.h2: Drawing Glasses in Different Art Styles
sections.content:
Cartoon/simple: Two circles or shapes connected by a line on the bridge. Exaggerate the shape for personality. Thick outlines, no internal detail needed.

Anime/manga: Glasses are often drawn with minimal bridge detail but strong lens reflections (the classic anime light-glare on glasses). Frame thickness varies by character — thin frames for intellectual characters, bold frames for quirky ones.

Realistic/portrait: Pay attention to material textures — the sheen of polished acetate, the matte finish of titanium, the slight colour variation in tortoiseshell. Use reference photos of real glasses for accuracy.

Fashion illustration: Stylize the frame shape to match the mood — oversized and bold for statement pieces, thin and minimal for understated elegance. Frame style can instantly communicate a character's personality.

Whatever style you're working in, real glasses make the best reference. If you want to study different frame shapes up close, stop by an optical store and look at the variety — from classic Ray-Ban styles to modern designer frames. Each shape has subtle proportional differences that make your drawings more authentic.

sections.h2: Practice Exercise: Draw Five Frame Styles
sections.content:
Grab a pencil and try drawing these five frame styles in a row, front view:

  1. Rectangular (simple, professional)
  2. Round (John Lennon style)
  3. Aviator (teardrop shape, double bridge)
  4. Cat-eye (upswept outer corners)
  5. Browline (thick top, thin bottom)

Draw each one three times: once as just the frames, once on a simple face outline, and once with details (reflections, shadows, thickness). By the time you've done all fifteen sketches, the proportions and shapes will feel natural.

Use real eyewear photos as reference. Look at how different frame brands handle proportions — a Ray-Ban Wayfarer has different curves than an Oakley or a Gucci frame. These subtle differences make illustrations feel specific and authentic.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

Your eyes are one of the most important tools you have, and they deserve proper care and correction. The technology behind modern eyewear has advanced remarkably in recent years — from digital freeform progressive lenses that customize the optics to your exact wearing position, to lightweight titanium frames that weigh less than a coin, to anti-reflective coatings that virtually eliminate glare. Understanding these options empowers you to make better choices about your eye health and get glasses that genuinely improve your quality of life rather than just meeting a basic need.

Regular eye exams are the foundation of good vision care. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends exams every one to two years for adults and annually for children, seniors over 65, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease. A comprehensive exam does far more than measure your prescription — your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels, catching conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration in their earliest stages. Many serious eye conditions have no noticeable symptoms until significant damage has occurred, making preventive exams essential.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What's the easiest frame style to draw?
faqs.a: Round glasses are the simplest — just two circles connected by a bridge. Rectangular frames are a close second. Both require minimal detail to look recognizable. Aviators and cat-eyes are slightly harder because the shapes are more specific.

faqs.q: How do you draw glasses from a side view?
faqs.a: From the side, you see one lens shape (appearing narrower due to perspective), the temple extending straight back to the ear, and the curve of the temple tip. The bridge is visible as a small protrusion from the lens edge. The other lens and temple are hidden behind the head.

faqs.q: How do you draw realistic lens reflections?
faqs.a: Add a curved white highlight near the upper portion of each lens. For sunglasses, add a gradient tint that's darkest at the top and lighter at the bottom, with the highlight left unshaded. A small secondary reflection near the bottom adds extra realism.

faqs.q: What's the best reference for drawing glasses?
faqs.a: Real glasses are the best reference. Visit an optical store like Charm Optical to see different frame styles up close, or search eyewear brand websites for high-resolution photos showing front, side, and three-quarter views of different frame types.

ctaTitle: Need Inspiration? See Real Frames Up Close
ctaText: Charm Optical in South Edmonton carries hundreds of frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Gucci, Oakley, and more. Whether you're an artist looking for reference or someone who needs new glasses, the team is happy to help.
